sanskrit: लोभः प्रवृत्तिरारम्भः कर्मणामशमः स्पृहा । रजस्येतानि जायन्ते विवृद्धे भरतर्षभ ॥
hindiTranslation: हे भरतवंशमें श्रेष्ठ अर्जुन  रजोगुणके बढ़नेपर लोभ, प्रवृत्ति, कर्मोंका आरम्भ, अशान्ति और स्पृहा  ये वृत्तियाँ पैदा होती हैं ॥
englishTranslation: O best of the Bharatas, when rajas grows, greed, activity, the beginning of actions, restlessness, and desire arise.
essence: Restless wanting multiplies action, but never settles the mind.
speaker: Krishna
simpleMeaning: When rajas becomes strong, greed, restless activity, constant starting, and craving appear.
simpleInsight: You can recognize agitation early, before it turns into compulsive wanting.
detailedMeaning: On the battlefield of Kurukshetra, Arjuna stands frozen while Krishna explains how the three gunas shape human behavior. After describing sattva, Krishna now shows Arjuna the marks of rajas: the force that drives craving, motion, and unsettled action.
modernRelevance: You open five tabs, start three tasks, and still feel unsatisfied. That buzzing urge to keep doing more is not productivity — it is restlessness taking over.
translationLiteral: लोभः (greed) / प्रवृत्तिः (activity, urge) / आरम्भः (beginning) / कर्मणाम् (of actions) / अशमः (restlessness, lack of peace) / स्पृहा (desire, craving) / रजसि (in rajas) / एतानि (these) / जायन्ते (are born) / विवृद्धे (when increased) / भरतर्षभ (O best of the Bharatas)
insight: It teaches you to notice the signature of rajas in your own mind: urgency, craving, and unfinished motion.
